questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: C — Not too satisfied. The mechanisms of democracy exist but I've seen too many instances where the spirit of democracy — protection of minorities, free press, accountable governance — feels strained. Being pragmatic and case-by-case, I can't say 'very satisfied,' but I also won't say 'not at all' since elections still happen and change is still possible.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy in India has real foundations — huge voter turnout, a functioning constitution — but I see too many warning signs: shrinking press freedom, economic disparity, communal tensions, and institutions under strain. As a moderate who looks at things pragmatically, I can't give it higher marks right now.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy is still intact in the sense that elections happen and I can vote, which matters. But I'm not satisfied with how it's working in practice. Economic disparities are growing, common people's struggles aren't adequately addressed, and I sometimes worry about whether institutions are strong enough. I'm pragmatic — I don't pick a strong partisan side — but I hold the system to account based on outcomes for people like me.

C — Not too satisfied. I believe in democracy and I vote every time. But right now, the way it's working feels disconnected from people like me. The flood took our savings and the system was slow to respond. I see partisanship drowning out real issues. I'm not giving up on democracy, but I can't honestly say I'm satisfied with how it's working.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy is functioning at a basic level — elections occur, transfers of power happen, courts exist. But I have real concerns about the quality of discourse, institutional pressures, and whether minority voices are adequately protected. I'm not very satisfied, but I'm not ready to say not at all satisfied either.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat unfavorable. I can acknowledge economic achievements and some genuine governance improvements, but the religious nationalism, minority treatment concerns, and press freedom issues push me toward unfavorable. As a pragmatic moderate, I can't ignore these issues, especially given my faith background.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I can acknowledge some policy efforts but the concerns around democratic norms, religious polarization, and minority rights outweigh the positives for me. My pragmatic, moderate instincts make me uncomfortable with strong nationalist rhetoric regardless of which country or party it comes from.

I'd say somewhat unfavorable — B or C. The reports about religious tension, minority rights concerns, and democratic institutions being weakened bother me. As a person of faith myself, I don't like when religion is weaponized politically against others. But I also recognize he has strong support and has done some economic work. I'll go with C — somewhat unfavorable — because the concerns about democratic norms and minority treatment outweigh the positives I've heard.

I'll say somewhat unfavorable — not because I dismiss everything he's done, but because on balance, his tenure has created more social division than unity, and economic policies haven't meaningfully helped households like mine. I'm pragmatic, not partisan, but the net assessment leans negative for me personally.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has delivered on some pragmatic fronts — infrastructure, digital economy, some foreign policy wins — but I have genuine hesitations about the handling of social divisions, press freedom concerns I've read about from multiple sources, and some economic policy missteps. Net-net, slightly positive but with significant reservations. That's 'somewhat favorable' territory for me.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: I'd say somewhat unfavorable. I can acknowledge governance achievements and don't want to be blindly partisan, but the treatment of religious minorities and the stronger Hindu nationalist elements of the party conflict with my values around religious freedom and community harmony. It's not 'very unfavorable' because I try to be fair and case-by-case.

I'd lean somewhat unfavorable based on what I've read about religious nationalism and reported tensions with minority communities under BJP governance — values that conflict with my own faith-based emphasis on human dignity. But I hold this view lightly given my limited knowledge.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not going to say 'very unfavorable' because I don't know enough from firsthand experience, and I try not to be extreme in my judgments. But based on what I've read and cross-referenced, their nationalist religious approach concerns me enough that I lean unfavorable.

Somewhat unfavorable — C. As a moderate, I can see some merits, but the divisive identity politics and concerns about communal harmony push me toward mild unfavorability. The economic policies haven't substantially helped people in my income bracket either.

Somewhat unfavorable — leaning slightly negative primarily because their track record on social cohesion and handling of minority communities concerns me more than their economic policies impress me. But it's genuinely close to neutral. I reject strong partisan labels, so this is a slight lean, not a firm verdict.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — I appreciate Congress's historical contributions to India's independence and some social programs, but the dynastic politics, corruption scandals, and perceived disconnect from ordinary people push me toward a mildly critical view. As a pragmatic moderate, I can't fully embrace or fully condemn, but on balance I lean slightly unfavorable.

I'd land on 'somewhat unfavorable' — not because I strongly oppose them, but because what I do know raises mild concerns: dynastic politics, some corruption scandals I've heard mentioned, and questions about whether they truly represent ordinary working people. But I hold this lightly — I don't know enough to be more certain.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly so, but the dynastic politics, reported corruption scandals, and mixed governance record give me pause. I value pragmatism and accountability, and from what I've read, there are real concerns there. But I hold this lightly since I'm not deeply informed.

Somewhat unfavorable — I appreciate some of Congress's historical contributions like rural employment and food security programs, but the corruption scandals, dynastic politics, and failure to consistently deliver for ordinary struggling families like mine leave me skeptical. I'm a pragmatic moderate, not strongly partisan, but on balance I lean slightly unfavorable.

Somewhat unfavorable. The historical legacy earns some respect, but the corruption scandals, dynastic politics, and lack of strong policy vision in recent years tip me toward unfavorable. It's not 'very' unfavorable — I can see policy areas where they've done reasonable work — but on balance I'm more critical than supportive right now.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. The evidence points clearly in that direction: economic growth, diplomatic influence, technological achievements, and strategic positioning between East and West. India is being courted by multiple global powers, which itself signals growing influence.
A
A

A — Getting stronger. India seems to be asserting itself more — economically, diplomatically, technologically. The space program success, G20 hosting, the growing diaspora influence, and how other countries now negotiate with India all point to a stronger global position.

A — Getting stronger. The weight of evidence from credible sources, economic data, diplomatic activity, and scientific achievements supports this. I hold this with nuance — there are real challenges — but the trajectory is upward.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat good — B. I want to say 'very good' but I'm a pragmatist. India's democracy has real problems: corruption, misinformation, identity-based voting, gridlock. So 'somewhat good' captures that it's the right system but with serious implementation challenges that prevent me from rating it 'very good.'

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is the right framework for India — it honors the diversity of the country and gives citizens agency. But I can't say 'very good' because in practice I see too many ways it falls short: corruption, money in politics, and poor communities like mine often being overlooked despite voting. The system is good in principle but needs real improvement in practice.

I would say somewhat good. Representative democracy gives citizens a voice and accountability mechanisms, but it comes with real challenges — corruption, representation gaps, money influencing politics. For India with its enormous diversity and scale, it's genuinely the best practical option even if imperfect. I won't say 'very good' because I've seen and read enough to know the system has serious flaws in practice.

Somewhat good. Representative democracy gives people a voice and creates accountability, but I can't say 'very good' because I've seen how money, caste, and corruption can distort it. The system has real flaws that hurt ordinary people. But compared to alternatives, it's still valuable — especially for people who have no other recourse.

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is genuinely good for India in principle, but I can't say 'very good' given real structural problems: corruption, money power in elections, communal polarization, weak institutional capacity in some states. It's the best available system but implementation has serious gaps. So 'somewhat good' captures my pragmatic, nuanced view.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: D — Very bad. A leader without parliament or elections is authoritarian by definition. India's strength comes from its democratic traditions. No matter how 'strong' or capable a leader, removing electoral accountability removes the people's ability to correct mistakes or remove abuse. This is very bad.

D — Very bad. Removing elections and parliament from governance removes the one thing that gives ordinary people any leverage over those in power. It's a recipe for corruption and abuse, especially for people like me who are already vulnerable economically and socially.

D — Very bad. Bypassing elections and parliament removes the only real protection ordinary citizens have. I'm politically moderate and I genuinely evaluate things case by case, but this isn't a close call. Democratic accountability is fundamental.

D — Very bad. No parliament, no elections means no accountability. Even if a leader starts with good intentions, there's nothing to stop them from going wrong. India's democracy, with all its messiness, gives people like me a voice. I'm not willing to trade that away.

D — Very bad. India's federal, democratic, parliamentary structure exists precisely to prevent one person or group from having unchecked power. History, expert consensus, and my own values all point the same direction here.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. While India shows strong GDP numbers on paper, the lived experience for most middle-class and lower-income families involves rising inflation, healthcare costs, and economic anxiety. As a pragmatist, I see both positives and negatives, but the negatives feel more pressing for ordinary people right now.

C — Somewhat bad. While India shows strong growth metrics, the lived reality for many ordinary people involves inflation, job insecurity, and widening inequality. I lean toward 'somewhat bad' because I tend to weight everyday economic hardship more than headline GDP numbers.

C — Somewhat bad. Growth numbers may look okay on paper, but for people like me — struggling with bills, recovering from financial hardship — the economy doesn't feel good. Inflation, job insecurity, and inequality make day-to-day life hard for ordinary families.

C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I do see some people doing better, some businesses growing, and I try to be balanced and not extreme in my views. But for people like me, the everyday economic reality feels strained. So 'somewhat bad' is honest and fair.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a pragmatic moderate; I won't say 'very bad' because there are real bright spots (tech sector, infrastructure investment, some GDP growth), but on balance, the everyday economic experience for middle-class families like mine feels strained. Inflation, quality-of-life costs, and employment concerns tip it toward 'somewhat bad' for me.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. I trust the national government to do some things right, but I hold serious reservations about accountability, institutional integrity, and whether the most vulnerable are truly protected. Faith teaches me to hope, but experience teaches me to verify.

B — Somewhat. I think the national government of India tries to do right by the country in some areas, but has significant failures and blind spots, especially for those with fewer resources. Faith matters to me — I try not to be purely cynical — but I've also seen how decisions made far away can hurt ordinary people. 'Somewhat' feels honest.

B — Somewhat. I trust the national government to do what is right sometimes, but not consistently. There are real achievements, but also serious failures especially for struggling families. My trust is guarded and conditional.

B — Somewhat. I'm a moderate by nature. I don't want to be extreme in either direction. The government does some things right, some things wrong. I've seen real failures — like after the flood — but also genuine efforts. 'Somewhat' captures my honest, nuanced position.

B — Somewhat. This captures my genuine, case-by-case moderate position. I trust the national government to do right on some issues and some of the time, but not consistently enough to say 'a lot,' and not so poorly that I'd say 'not much.'

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. Despite real challenges like inequality and environmental pressures, India's overall trajectory of economic growth, expanding education access, and technological opportunity gives the next generation a better foundation than their parents had. I hold this with nuance — it won't be universal, and some will be left behind — but on balance, the trend is upward.

A — Better off. Despite real challenges like inequality, climate stress, and uncertain job markets, India's trajectory over recent decades has been upward for many families. Education access, technology, and economic growth give me reason to believe children today will have more opportunities than their parents, even if it won't be easy.

A — Better off. Despite my personal hardships and the very real risks, I believe the trajectory for children in India today points upward. More access to education, digital opportunities, and a growing economy give them a real chance at more financial stability than their parents had — though it's not guaranteed for everyone.

A — Better off. Cautiously, with hesitation. I believe in India's potential and in the resilience of the next generation. Faith and community teach me hope. But I recognize this is far from certain — many children, especially from poor families like mine, may not share in that progress equally.

A — Better off. India's economic fundamentals, expanding digital economy, and historical generational improvement trend suggest children today will likely have more financial opportunity than their parents. However, I hold this view with caveats — inequality, climate impacts, and cost of living pressures are real concerns that could undermine the headline numbers for many families.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Religion isn't just a Sunday habit for me. It shapes my worldview, my relationships, my sense of purpose, and how I navigate suffering and joy alike.
A — Very important. Faith is not just one part of my life; it's the foundation everything else rests on.
A — Very important. There's no hesitation here. Religion is not just somewhat important to me — it is the core of my daily life, my values, how I raise my family, and how I endure hardship.
A — Very important. Faith is not peripheral to my life, it IS my life's foundation.

A — Very important. Religion and faith are genuinely central to my daily life. From morning prayers to how I approach big decisions to how I raise my children — faith is the foundation, not an add-on.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: Somewhat disagree — leaning toward completely disagree. I hold nuanced views, and I don't want to entirely dismiss the value of deference in certain circumstances within a loving partnership, but 'must always obey' is far too strong and absolutist. That phrasing removes agency entirely, which I cannot accept.
somewhat_disagree

C — Somewhat disagree. I won't go to 'completely disagree' because I do believe in honoring and respecting my spouse, and in my faith there is value in humility within marriage. But 'always obey' is too absolute — marriage requires mutual respect, not one-sided submission.

C — Somewhat disagree. I stop short of 'completely disagree' because I do believe in mutual respect within marriage, and there are moments of deference that come from love and wisdom, not coercion. But 'always obey' goes too far. Marriage is a partnership.

C — Somewhat disagree. I want to be honest: I lean toward D (completely disagree) because of the word 'always,' but I acknowledge that in some faith traditions there is a concept of the husband as head of household, which I partially respect as a cultural reality. So I land at somewhat disagree rather than completely, because I don't want to dismiss all traditional family structures outright. But mandatory, unconditional obedience — no.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: Completely disagree. There is no scenario where gender alone should determine who deserves employment. Jobs should go to the most qualified person, regardless of gender.

D — Completely disagree. Gender shouldn't determine who has more right to a job. What matters is skill, need, and qualification. I've been in tight situations where I needed work badly. Nobody should be pushed out based on gender.
D — Completely disagree. Jobs should be based on qualifications, need, and merit — not gender. Women support families too, and denying them equal access to work is both unfair and harmful.
D — Completely disagree. Jobs should be based on merit and need, not gender. Scarcity doesn't justify discrimination. I've lived through scarcity, and women's work matters just as much.

C — Somewhat disagree. I lean toward completely disagree, but I acknowledge there may be rare, specific cultural or contextual arguments people make, so I'll stop at 'somewhat' to reflect my moderate temperament. But my core position is that merit and need should determine employment, not gender.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is very important. This isn't a partisan position; it's a foundational human rights stance consistent with my faith, my values, and my experience as a woman and mother.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India, or anywhere, matter deeply. My faith teaches me every person has inherent worth. My own life experiences remind me how hard it is when systems don't treat you fairly. This isn't a partisan issue for me — it's a human issue.

A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men in India is very important. Equal rights mean women can protect their families, access education, work, make decisions, and live with dignity. As someone who has had to carry my family through crisis, I know firsthand that women need equal standing to do what life demands of them.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights in India is very important to me. This isn't partisan; it's about basic human dignity, safety, and fairness. I've lived close enough to vulnerability to know that when women lack rights, entire families and communities suffer.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is not just a political position for me; it's grounded in my pragmatic worldview and my faith values. I've seen how inequality creates inefficiency and suffering. This is one of those clear cases where being case-by-case still lands firmly in one place.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. India is uniquely exposed: a long coastline, heavy dependence on monsoons for agriculture, dense population, and growing water stress. I may not align with every extreme policy position, but the evidence of real harm to real people — especially the poor and farming communities — is too strong to call this minor.

A — Major threat. India's sheer scale of population, agricultural dependency, coastal exposure, and documented extreme weather events make this feel like a major threat, not a minor one. I'm cautious about overstating things, but the evidence here points clearly in one direction.
A

A — Major threat. India is one of the most climate-vulnerable countries in the world. We have massive coastlines at risk, a billion-plus people dependent on the monsoon for food and water, extreme heat events, and glacier-fed rivers that are changing. Having experienced a devastating flood myself, I cannot in good conscience call this minor.

A — Major threat. The expert consensus is clear, I notice local effects, and India's agriculture, water supply, and coastal populations face serious risks. My moderate, pragmatic stance doesn't mean ignoring strong evidence.
